features.description:
## Neuron Pattern Description

This neuron detects tokens related to **Christian religious concepts**, specifically the words "faith" and "Christian" (including as part of compound words like "ChristianNewswire"). The activating tokens are semantically tied to Christianity and religious belief, appearing in contexts involving Christian practice, doctrine, healing, community, and religious identity.

## Pattern Description

This neuron detects tokens related to **professional certification, credentialing, and qualification verification** — specifically words like "certified," "certification," and "verifies" that appear in contexts describing formal professional credentials, licensing requirements, renewal processes, and board certifications across various fields (healthcare, coaching, IT support management, etc.).

The neuron appears to activate on tokens that appear within contexts describing **indigenous, tribal, or ancient peoples and their cultural practices/artifacts**. The marked tokens themselves are varied grammatically (prepositions, adjectives, conjunctions), but they consistently occur within passages about native/indigenous communities, traditional crafts, ancient civilizations, or aboriginal cultural heritage.

The neuron activates on the word "New" (or "new") when it appears at the **beginning of a phrase, headline, title, or list item** — specifically when "New" functions as the first word introducing a new topic, section header, proper noun phrase, or announcement. The token is consistently in a sentence-initial or heading-initial position, often capitalized.

## Pattern Description

This neuron detects **infinitive verb phrases following the word "To"** — specifically, the base-form verb that immediately follows "To" at the start of an infinitive construction (e.g., "To **link**," "To **access**," "To **go**," "To **find**"). The pattern is syntactic: the marked token is always the main verb of a purpose-expressing infinitive clause, typically appearing in instructional, navigational, or call-to-action contexts.

prediction_details.predicted: I need to find the token that is the main verb of a purpose-expressing infinitive clause following "To" in each excerpt.
Excerpt 1: "to make it even better" — "make" follows "to
Excerpt 2: "to use as its new headquarters" — "use" follows "to
Excerpt 3: "to appeal to all women" — "appeal" follows "to

Excerpt 5: "to get in touch" / "to say hello" / "to hear from you" — "get" is first, but "say" and "hear" also qualify. The most call-to-action/purpose one: "say" or "hear". Looking at context: "to say hello" and "to hear from you" — "say" seems most prominent as it's in a list.
Excerpt 6: "to choose from" / "to put on them" / "to use" — "use" at the end seems most prominent as it's the final purpose clause.

features.description: The neuron detects **first-person performative speech act verbs** (say, admit, warn, promise) that follow a subject pronoun and introduce a personal statement or disclosure. The neuron detects tokens that are part of **verbs describing physical locomotion or movement through space** (walking, marching, strolling, running, rocketing, sprinting, etc.). The neuron detects **hedging/approximation adverbs** — words like "less," "virtually," "much," "of," "basically," "pretty," and "fairly" that soften or qualify a statement, indicating something is approximate, near-complete, or not quite absolute. These tokens all function as degree modifiers or qualifiers that signal imprecision or partial truth.

## Pattern Description

This neuron detects **time duration expressions**, specifically tokens that are part of quantified time periods (days, hours, years, months, or numeric quantities preceding time units). The marked tokens consistently appear as components of phrases specifying a measurable span of time (e.g., "3 days," "48 hours," "10 years," "5 years," "2 years," "60 day").

The neuron detects words related to the concept of **entry/entrance/input** — specifically tokens that are morphological variants or synonyms of "entry" (entry, entrance, entrances, input), all sharing the semantic notion of a point of access, submission, or beginning.

The neuron detects the expletive/dummy pronoun "it" followed by a linking verb (typically "is" or "'s"), specifically in the construction "it is/it's" where "it" functions as a formal subject introducing an adjective complement or subordinate clause (e.g., "it's important that...", "it is fundamental to...").
